title: საქართველოს სამოქალაქო კოდექსი — სასაქონლო საიდუმლოება და ისმა კანონი
title_en: Trade Secrets Protection in Georgian Law (Civil Code and Law on Unfair Competition)
description: Trade secret protection in Georgia is established through multiple legal instruments, primarily the Civil Code of Georgia, the Law on Unfair Competition, and provisions in the Criminal Code. The framework aligns with the EU Trade Secrets Directive (Directive (EU) 2016/943) as part of Georgia's EU approximation commitments. Georgia's trade secret framework provides both civil and criminal remedies for the misappropriation of commercially valuable confidential information.

provisions.title: Article 1. Definition of Trade Secret
provisions.content: A trade secret (commercial secret) is any information that is: (a) secret, in the sense that it is not, as a body or in the precise configuration and assembly of its components, generally known among or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question; (b) has commercial value because it is secret; and (c) has been subject to reasonable steps under the circumstances, by the person lawfully in control of the information, to keep it secret. Trade secrets may include technical information such as manufacturing processes, formulas, and designs, as well as commercial information such as business plans, customer lists, pricing strategies, and financial projections. Georgia's definition of trade secrets is aligned with Article 2 of Directive (EU) 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (Trade Secrets Directive) and Article 39 of the TRIPS Agreement. Reference: Directive (EU) 2016/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on the protection of trade secrets.

provisions.title: Article 2. Unlawful Acquisition and Disclosure
provisions.content: The following conduct constitutes unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets: (a) the acquisition of trade secrets without consent through unauthorized access to, appropriation of, or copying of any documents, objects, materials, substances, or electronic files that contain the trade secrets; (b) any other conduct which, under the circumstances, is considered contrary to honest commercial practices; (c) the use or disclosure of a trade secret where the trade secret was acquired by unlawful means; (d) the use or disclosure of a trade secret in breach of a confidentiality agreement or any other duty to limit the use or disclosure of the trade secret; (e) the use or disclosure of a trade secret by a person who exceeded the scope of an agreement or a duty to limit the use or disclosure of the trade secret. Acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret shall also be unlawful where a person, at the time of acquisition, use, or disclosure, knew or ought to have known that the trade secret had been obtained directly or indirectly from another person who was using or disclosing the trade secret unlawfully.

provisions.title: Article 3. Civil Remedies
provisions.content: The holder of a trade secret whose rights have been violated may request from the court: (a) cessation of or, where appropriate, a prohibition of the use or disclosure of the trade secret; (b) a prohibition of the production, offering, placing on the market, or use of infringing goods, or the importation, export, or storage of infringing goods for those purposes; (c) the adoption of the appropriate corrective measures with regard to the infringing goods; (d) the destruction of all or part of any document, object, material, substance, or electronic file containing or implementing the trade secret; (e) compensation for the actual prejudice suffered as a result of the unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of the trade secret, including lost profits and unjust enrichment; and (f) publication of judicial decisions. Where the unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret was carried out unintentionally, courts may, in appropriate cases, order the payment of pecuniary compensation instead of applying the measures referred to above, if their application would cause disproportionate harm to the infringer and if such compensation appears to be satisfactory.

provisions.title: Article 4. Interim and Provisional Measures
provisions.content: Courts may, at the request of the trade secret holder, order immediate interim measures to: (a) cease or prohibit the use or disclosure of the trade secret on a provisional basis; (b) prohibit the production, offering, placing on the market, or use of infringing goods; (c) seize or deliver up the suspected infringing goods to prevent their entry into, or circulation on, the market. Courts may also, as an alternative, make the continuation of the alleged unlawful use of a trade secret subject to the provision of security intended to ensure the compensation of the trade secret holder. Where the interim measures are withdrawn or lapse due to any act or omission of the trade secret holder, or where the court finds that there has been no unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret, courts may order the trade secret holder to provide the defendant with appropriate compensation for any injury caused by those measures.

provisions.title: Article 5. Exceptions to Trade Secret Protection
provisions.content: Trade secret protection shall not apply where acquisition of the trade secret was achieved by: (a) independent discovery or creation; (b) observation, study, disassembly or testing of a product or object that has been made available to the public or that is lawfully in the possession of the acquirer of the information, where the acquirer is free from any legally valid duty to limit the acquisition of the trade secret; (c) exercise of the right of workers or workers' representatives to information and consultation; and (d) any other practice which, under the circumstances, is in conformity with honest commercial practices. Disclosure and acquisition of a trade secret shall be lawful to the extent that such disclosure or acquisition is required or allowed by law. In particular, the measures, procedures, and remedies provided for in this Law shall not be applied where the alleged acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret was carried out for exercising the right to freedom of expression and information, for revealing misconduct or illegal activity for the purpose of protecting the general public interest.

provisions.title: Article 6. Confidentiality of Trade Secrets in Judicial Proceedings
provisions.content: In legal proceedings relating to the unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret, courts and relevant authorities may, on a duly reasoned application by a party, take the specific measures necessary to preserve the confidentiality of any trade secret or alleged trade secret used or referred to in the course of legal proceedings. Such measures shall include: (a) restricting access to documents containing trade secrets or alleged trade secrets submitted by the parties or third parties; (b) restricting access to hearings; (c) making available to third parties a non-confidential version of any judicial decision in which passages containing trade secrets have been removed or redacted. Courts shall balance the need for confidentiality against the right to an effective remedy and a fair trial, and shall ensure that parties' rights to access documents necessary for the proceedings are not unduly restricted.

provisions.title: Article 7. Criminal Liability for Trade Secret Theft
provisions.content: The Criminal Code of Georgia establishes criminal liability for the unlawful disclosure or use of trade secrets. A person who, for purposes of competition, discloses or uses without authorization a trade secret obtained in circumstances involving a duty of confidentiality, or obtained through unlawful means including unauthorized access to computer systems, shall be punishable by a fine or imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years. Where the offense is committed: (a) by a person in a position of trust within an enterprise; (b) for the benefit of a foreign state or foreign entity; (c) causing substantial economic damage; (d) by an organized criminal group; the penalty shall be imprisonment for a term of three to eight years. Corporate espionage involving the use of electronic surveillance, cyberattacks, or the recruitment of insiders to obtain trade secrets shall be subject to enhanced penalties, in line with Georgia's commitments under the Budapest Convention and the EU Trade Secrets Directive.

provisions.title: Article 8. Trade Secrets in the Workplace
provisions.content: Employees and former employees shall maintain the confidentiality of trade secrets acquired in the course of their employment. Non-disclosure agreements protecting trade secrets are valid and enforceable in Georgian law, subject to the following conditions: (a) the agreement must clearly specify the information that is to be treated as confidential; (b) the agreement must not prevent the employee from using general skills and knowledge acquired in the employment; and (c) the agreement must not unreasonably restrict the employee's ability to earn a living after leaving the employment. Post-employment non-compete clauses that serve to protect trade secrets are enforceable where they are reasonable in scope, duration, and geographic area, and where they are supported by adequate consideration. In assessing reasonableness, courts shall take into account the employee's access to trade secrets and the potential harm to the employer from the use of those secrets by competitors.

provisions.title: Article 9. Trade Secrets and Procurement
provisions.content: Where a private entity submits information constituting trade secrets to a public body in connection with a procurement procedure, regulatory application, or other official proceeding, the public body shall take measures to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of such information. Public bodies shall: (a) apply appropriate access restrictions to trade secret information; (b) refuse requests for access to trade secrets information under the Law on Freedom of Information; and (c) take disciplinary action against officials who improperly disclose trade secrets submitted in official proceedings. Public bodies shall not use trade secret information obtained in official proceedings for commercial purposes or share it with competitors of the submitting entity. Compensation shall be payable to the holder of trade secrets where a public body improperly discloses trade secret information.

provisions.title: Article 10. EU Alignment
provisions.content: This Law shall be interpreted in a manner consistent with Directive (EU) 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure, as part of Georgia's obligations under the EU-Georgia Association Agreement and the Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area (DCFTA). Pending the completion of Georgia's EU accession process, Georgian courts and authorities shall take into account the interpretation of the Trade Secrets Directive by the Court of Justice of the European Union in applying the corresponding provisions of Georgian law. The National Intellectual Property Center of Georgia (Sakpatenti) shall monitor EU developments in trade secret law and propose amendments to Georgian legislation as necessary to maintain alignment with the EU acquis. Reference: Directive (EU) 2016/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on the protection of trade secrets.

definitions.definition: Information that is secret, has commercial value because it is secret, and has been subject to reasonable steps to keep it secret by the person lawfully in control of the information.
Goods whose design, characteristics, functioning, production process, or marketing significantly benefits from trade secrets unlawfully acquired, used or disclosed.
